extracted.brandBackground: Alloy offers personal training in a group setting delivered by certified instructors under the Alloy name.

leadership.name: Rick Mayo
leadership.background: Founder and CEO since inception in May 2019; operated an Alloy Facility since November 1992.
leadership.whyItMatters: Brings over three decades of direct brand operating experience.

leadership.name: Suzanne Robb
leadership.background: COO since inception in May 2019; held same title with affiliate since May 2011.
leadership.whyItMatters: Ensures operational continuity and long-term systems management.

leadership.name: Matt Helland
leadership.background: VP of Club Operations since May 2019; held same title with affiliate since May 2011.
leadership.whyItMatters: Directs day-to-day club support and training execution.

leadership.name: Jared Breen
leadership.background: VP since January 2023; previously served as Lead Franchise Business Coach.
leadership.whyItMatters: Manages site selection and facility build-out processes.

leadership.name: Chamberlynn Campuzano
leadership.background: VP since June 2024; previously Executive Assistant to COO and CEO.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees corporate administrative and operational systems.

lineItems.notes: Paid upon signing the Franchise Agreement.
Estimated lease payments for the first three months.
Security deposits required by landlord and utility companies.
Fees for design, layout, and architectural drawings.
Costs to build out the facility space.
Includes reception desk, exercise equipment, and fixtures.
Bottled water, protein drinks, and branded apparel.
Pre-sale and grand opening marketing campaign.
Travel, lodging, and meals for training.
Business cards, brochures, and basic supplies.
Chamber dues, towels, and cleaning supplies.
Working capital for the first three months.

hiddenCosts.name: Technology Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $286 per week for CRM, POS, Scheduler, Alloy App, and reporting dashboard.

hiddenCosts.name: Bookkeeping Service Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Mandatory monthly bookkeeping service fee paid to required vendor.

hiddenCosts.name: Redex Camera/Security System Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Mandatory monthly security system fee paid to required vendor.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: Disputes must be resolved by mediation, arbitration, and/or litigation only in Georgia.

operationalRisks.title: Sales Performance Required
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must maintain minimum sales performance levels to retain territorial rights.

operationalRisks.title: Supplier Control
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must purchase nearly all inventory and supplies from designated suppliers.

extracted.conceptRationale: Alloy is a personal training fitness studio offering group training programs.
extracted.staffingRationale: Requires a General Manager/Head Trainer and certified instructors to deliver group-train clients.

riskReasons: Rent is heavy: ~26% of mid-cohort revenue (above 25%).
A high-severity operational tripwire is disclosed — see the tripwires section.

underwriting.assessment: With $250,000 liquid against a $403,387 mid-point build-out, there is a $153,387 capital gap. An SBA loan or an equity partner is required to do this deal. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $16,602/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: Alloy is a personal training fitness studio offering group training programs.

benchmark.label: Boutique fitness / studio
benchmark.dominantRisk: Member retention/churn and fixed-cost coverage — NOT cost of goods. The deal is decided by recurring membership against rent and equipment lease.
benchmark.rampNote: 9–18 months to a mature, retained member base.
considerations: COGS is trivial; the risks are member churn and covering fixed rent + equipment lease.
Ask Item 20 operators for monthly retention, average membership lifetime, and break-even member count.
Model CAC and free-trial conversion — member acquisition cost is the hidden operating line.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 15–28% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: conventionally staffed · ≈ 2.1–3.1 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Boutique fitness / studio” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Taken from the FDD (~$9,208/mo); already netted in margin after fees & rent.
Estimated at the 3–10% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 20–30% conventionally staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: The franchisor flagged its own financial condition as a risk. The figures we extracted look comparatively stable, so confirm directly against the most recent statements.
financialCondition.context: Worth perspective: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt; revenue is growing. Early-stage franchisors commonly run losses and carry a deficit while investing to scale — this reads more like spending ahead of revenue than a failing business. The real question is runway: how the gap is funded, and for how long.
body: Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut. There is no parent guarantee of performance in this FDD, so Alloy Inter HoldCo, LLC is not contractually obligated to keep funding the franchisor or backing its commitments to you.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
aggravators: no parent guarantee backing the franchisor's obligations to you
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
